pattern: Rank points by squared distance (no square root needed) and keep the k smallest, which a partial selection or a size-k heap finds without fully sorting.
hints: Comparing squared distances gives the same ordering as true Euclidean distance but stays in integer arithmetic.
You never need the points beyond the k closest sorted, so a heap of size k or a quickselect partition suffices.
Sort all points by x*x + y*y and slice the first k (or maintain a max-heap of k by negative squared distance). The k smallest squared distances are the answer in any order.

edge_inputs.description: k equals number of points returns all

stress.description: random points with distinct squared distances so the closest-k set is unique
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    seen = set()
    pts = []
    while len(pts) < n:
        x = rng.randint(-10000, 10000)
        y = rng.randint(-10000, 10000)
        d = x * x + y * y
        if d in seen:
            continue
        seen.add(d)
        pts.append([x, y])
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [pts, k]
stress.note: distinct squared distances guarantee a unique closest-k multiset
